topic: Logic and computability of the Collatz conjecture: Conway's unpredictable iterations, Kurtz-Simon Pi^0_2-completeness, arithmetic-hierarchy classification, De Mol tag systems, independence/unsettleability, and Michel's busy-beaver/Collatz-like program

key_facts.fact: Conway's 1972 theorem (verbatim): 'If f is any computable function, there is a function g such that (1) g(n)/n is periodic (with rational values), (2) 2^f(n) = g^k(2^n), where k is minimal positive subject to g^k(2^n) a power of 2.' Corollary (verbatim): 'There is no algorithm, which, given a function g with g(n)/n periodic, and given a number n, determines whether or not there is k with g^k(n) = 1.' Here g ranges over maps g(n) = a_i n + b_i for n ≡ i (mod P) with rational a_i, b_i chosen so g(n) is always integral; the theorem holds even in the purely multiplicative case b_i = 0.
key_facts.source: J. H. Conway, 'Unpredictable Iterations', Proc. Number Theory Conference, Univ. of Colorado, Boulder, 1972, pp. 49-52; reprinted with commentary pp. 219-223 in J. C. Lagarias (ed.), The Ultimate Challenge: The 3x+1 Problem, AMS 2010. Scan: https://gwern.net/doc/cs/computable/1972-conway.pdf

key_facts.fact: Conway's proof chain: Minsky register machines -> 'vector games' (add first vector from a finite list preserving nonnegativity) -> 'rational games' (replace n by r_i·n for the least i making it an integer), via encoding a register vector (a,b,c,...) as 2^a 3^b 5^c.... For any computable f there is a rational game started at 2^n that reaches 2^f(n) without passing any intermediate power of 2. This model was formalized as FRACTRAN in Conway 1987 ('FRACTRAN: A Simple Universal Computing Language for Arithmetic', in Open Problems in Communication and Computation, Springer, pp. 3-27). Conway explicitly notes the caveat: 'our theorem says nothing about the Collatz game. But it does prohibit any general solution to games of this type, and also shows that there exist special cases for which the prediction problem is unsolvable.'
key_facts.source: Conway 1972, pp. 220-221 of the Lagarias-volume reprint (downloaded PDF), and editorial commentary in the same reprint

key_facts.fact: Kurtz-Simon definitions and theorems (from the authors' paper): a 'Collatz function' is a g with integer p >= 2 and rationals {a_i}, {b_i} such that x ≡ i (mod p) implies g(x) = a_i x + b_i is integral. Theorem 1.4: 'Given a Collatz function g, it is undecidable whether or not for all integers x there exists an i such that g^(i)(x) = 1.' Theorem 1.5 (stronger form): with range-g = {x : ∃i g^(i)(x) = 1}, the problem 'range-g = ω' is Π_2-complete (i.e., the set of generalized Collatz functions for which every positive integer eventually reaches 1 is Π^0_2-complete). They state Conway's theorem as: undecidable whether for all x of the form 2^k there exists i with g^(i)(x) = 1 (Theorem 1.6).
key_facts.source: S. A. Kurtz and J. Simon, 'The Undecidability of the Generalized Collatz Problem', TAMC 2007, LNCS 4484, pp. 542-553; author PDF https://people.cs.uchicago.edu/~simon/RES/collatz.pdf (downloaded)

key_facts.fact: Kurtz-Simon proof mechanism: Conway's construction only controls integers of the form 2^k (initial configurations), so its g's behave arbitrarily on 'garbage' configurations. Kurtz-Simon run a DOUBLE simulation of a register machine: an outer simulation run forward (halts iff the original machine halts) and an inner simulation that re-checks the outer one for consistency and always halts; Theorem 2.3: 'A register machine M can be effectively converted into a register machine M' such that M is total if and only if M' reaches a halting configuration from every configuration.' This converts totality (a Pi^0_2-complete property, tot = {e : phi_e total}) into all-orbits-reach-1 for a Collatz function, giving Pi^0_2-hardness; membership in Pi^0_2 is by inspection of the quantifier form.
key_facts.source: Kurtz & Simon 2007, Sections 1-2 of downloaded PDF (draft dated December 26, 2006)

key_facts.fact: Arithmetic-hierarchy classification of the Collatz conjecture itself: the full conjecture 'for all n>0 there exists k with T^k(n)=1' is a Pi^0_2 sentence (universal quantifier over n, existential over k, primitive recursive matrix) — this is exactly Problem 1.1 in Kurtz-Simon and the quantifier form ∀n∃k in every formalization. Component 'there is a nontrivial cycle' is Sigma^0_1 (a cycle is a finite, machine-verifiable witness), so 'no nontrivial cycles' is Pi^0_1. Component 'the orbit of n eventually enters a cycle' is Sigma^0_1 in n (∃i<j T^i(n)=T^j(n)), so 'no divergent orbits' is Pi^0_2. Kurtz-Simon's Theorem 1.5 shows Pi^0_2 is optimal at the level of the generalized problem (Pi^0_2-completeness).
key_facts.source: Kurtz & Simon 2007 (Problem 1.1, Theorem 1.5); classification of components is by direct quantifier count, corroborated by Conway 2013's discussion of counterexample types (Amer. Math. Monthly 120(3):192-198)

key_facts.fact: Independence consequences: for a Pi^0_1 sentence, independence from a sound theory implies truth. Conway 2013 states the mechanism verbatim (for FLT/Goldbach): 'Exhibiting these numbers proves the negation and disproves the theorem itself. So if one shows that the theorem is not disprovable, then one also shows there exist no such nth powers and therefore that the theorem is true.' This applies to the 'no nontrivial cycles' half of Collatz (Pi^0_1: a cycle would be a verifiable counterexample) but NOT to the full Pi^0_2 conjecture: a divergent orbit is an infinite object, and Conway 2013 notes 'In general, if a Collatzian game does not stop, then there is no proof of this.' Hence proving Collatz independent of PA or ZFC would not establish its truth — falsity via divergent orbit could itself be unprovable.
key_facts.source: J. H. Conway, 'On Unsettleable Arithmetical Problems', Amer. Math. Monthly 120(3), March 2013, pp. 192-198 (downloaded PDF, Sections 1 and 5); https://raganwald.com/assets/fractran/Conway-On-Unsettleable-Arithmetical-Problems.pdf

key_facts.fact: Conway 2013 exhibits an explicit 24-fraction FRACTRAN ('Collatzian') game that is unsettleable: 'There is an explicit game with 24 simple linear functions for which there are numbers n for which the game never stops, but this is not provable' — for any consistent axiom system defining 'settleable'. John Rickard (unpublished, d. 2002) improved this to 7 fractions. The construction: adjoin 1/2 to a 23-fraction universal game computing f(n)=0 iff the nth axiom system proves 0=1; the game stops at 1 precisely when the system is inconsistent.

Conway 2013 Postscript (added June 8, 2012), on Collatz itself: using arbitrarily tall 'mountains' (2^k m - 1 passes in 2k steps to 3^k m - 1, and by the Chinese Remainder Theorem 3^k m - 1 can be arranged to have the form 2^l n), he argues each googol-sized mountain has probability at least ~one googolth of closing into a cycle, and concludes: 'the fact that this probability, though very small, is positive, makes it extremely unlikely that there can be a proof that the Collatz game has no cycles that contain only large numbers'; the argument 'convinced me that the Collatz 3n+1 Conjecture is itself very likely to be unsettleable.' He also proposes the amusical permutation mu: 2k -> 3k, 4k+1 -> 3k+1, 4k-1 -> 3k-1 (a bijection), with the assertion '8 belongs to an infinite cycle' as the simplest statement he believes true but unsettleable, and coins 'probvious' (probabilistically obvious). Appendix 1 concedes a Hardy-Littlewood-circle-method-style proof of Collatz is 'not entirely inconceivable' since heuristics predict decrease.
key_facts.source: Conway 2013, Postscript p. 197-198 and Sections 4-5, Appendix 1

key_facts.fact: De Mol's tag-system reduction: the Collatz problem reduces to the 2-tag system (deletion number v=2) with 3 symbols and production rules a_0 -> a_1 a_2, a_1 -> a_0, a_2 -> a_0 a_0 a_0 (commonly written a -> bc, b -> a, c -> aaa), with n encoded in unary as a^n; the system computes iterates of the shortcut Collatz map (2n -> n via a^n -> (bc)^{n/2} -> a^{n/2}; odd n -> (3n+1)/2) and halts iff the orbit reaches 1 (halting on words of length < 2; the system has no halting symbol). Note l_max - v = v - l_min = 1, i.e., it sits at the exact decidability frontier of Wang's criteria. De Mol also extended the scheme to simulate an arbitrary generalized Collatz function of modulus d by a d-tag system. The Collatz problem had previously been reduced to small Turing machines by Baiocchi, Margenstern and Michel, 'however, the description of these machines is longer than that of the Collatz tag system.'
key_facts.source: L. De Mol, 'Tag systems and Collatz-like functions', Theoretical Computer Science 390(1), 2008, pp. 92-101, doi:10.1016/j.tcs.2007.10.020; restated with exact rules in L. De Mol, 'On the boundaries of solvability and unsolvability in tag systems', EPTCS 1, 2009, pp. 56-66, doi:10.4204/EPTCS.1.5 (arXiv:0906.3329, downloaded), Section 2.2

key_facts.fact: De Mol's decidability-frontier facts: Minsky 1961 proved tag systems unsolvable with deletion number v=6; Cocke-Minsky improved to v=2; Maslov: for any v>1 there is a tag system with unsolvable decision problem, while every v=1 tag system is solvable (also Post, Wang); Post proved TS(1,v) and TS(2,2) have solvable halting/reachability problems (TS(2,2) proof only recently reconstructed by De Mol via her 'table method'); universal tag systems exist in TS(480,2) and TS(608,2) via simulation of the small universal Turing machines TM(15,2) (Neary-Woods) and TM(19,2) (Baiocchi). De Mol: 'Given the complexities involved with the Collatz problem, it might thus be very hard to prove the class TS(3,2) recursively solvable.' Post's own TS(2,3) system (0 -> 00, 1 -> 1101, v=3) remains of unknown decidability since 1921; Post described the class as of 'bewildering complexity'; Asveld disproved a claimed counterexample: on A_0 = (100)^110 the system halts after 43,913,328,040,672 steps.

Michel's busy-beaver/Collatz program: P. Michel, 'Busy beaver competition and Collatz-like problems', Archive for Mathematical Logic 32(5), 1993, pp. 351-367, proved that the halting problems (on general inputs) of the then-record 5-state 2-symbol busy beaver machines (Schult 1983, Uhing 1984/86, Marxen-Buntrock 1989) are equivalent to open Collatz-like problems in number theory. In his LMCS survey he tabulates: the Marxen-Buntrock champion (sigma=4098, s=47,176,870) is 'pure Collatz-like (3 -> 5) without parameter, 3 rules, 15 transitions.' Formal definitions there: f is a generalized Collatz mapping iff there exist d>=2 and integers a_i, b_i with f(dn+i) = a_i n + b_i for all n and 0<=i<=d-1 (three equivalent formulations, Def. 1.1); a pure Collatz-like function is the partial version (undefined on one or more residues, Def. 1.2); exponential Collatz-like functions f(dn+i) = (a_i p^n + b_i)/c_i (Def. 1.5, 'currently, no study of this type of function is known').
key_facts.source: P. Michel, 'Problems in number theory from busy beaver competition', Logical Methods in Computer Science 11(4:10), 2015, pp. 1-35, doi:10.2168/LMCS-11(4:10)2015, arXiv:1311.1029 (downloaded); Michel 1993 via Springer https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF01409968

key_facts.fact: Exact Collatz-like map of the 5-state busy beaver champion (Marxen-Buntrock 1989, per Michel's analysis Section 5.2.1 as rendered by Aaronson): g(x) = (5x+18)/3 if x ≡ 0 (mod 3); (5x+22)/3 if x ≡ 1 (mod 3); ⊥ (halt) if x ≡ 2 (mod 3). Starting from x=0 the orbit is 0 -> 6 -> 16 -> 34 -> 64 -> 114 -> 196 -> 334 -> 564 -> 946 -> 1584 -> 2646 -> 4416 -> 7366 -> 12284 -> ⊥ (14 iterations), and each iteration costs ~(5/9)x^2 machine steps, yielding the 47,176,870-step runtime. BB(5) = 47,176,870 was finally PROVED by the bbchallenge Collaboration (Coq-verified, arXiv:2509.12337, 'Determination of the fifth Busy Beaver value'), settling all 5-state holdouts including Skelet's Collatz-like machines.
key_facts.source: S. Aaronson, 'The Busy Beaver Frontier', SIGACT News 51(3), 2020, https://www.scottaaronson.com/papers/bb.pdf (downloaded), Section 4; bbchallenge Collaboration, arXiv:2509.12337 (PDF in papers/)

key_facts.fact: Michel 2014 gives the smallest known Turing machines simulating the 3x+1 function T (T(2n)=n, T(2n+1)=3n+2): a never-halting 3-state 4-symbol machine M1 working in base 3 (Theorem 4.1: the 3x+1 conjecture is true iff for all x written in base 3, M1 eventually reaches configuration omega-b 0^n (A1) b-omega), and machines that HALT exactly on the final loop in classes 3x10, 4x6, 5x4, 13x2 — e.g., Theorem 5.3: 'The 3x+1 conjecture is true iff Turing machine M6 [5 states, 4 symbols] halts on all input x = x_k...x_0 representing a positive integer written in base 3.' Known simulators of general Collatz-like functions exist in classes 2x4, 3x3, 5x2; universal TMs exist in 2x18, 3x9, 4x6, 5x5, 6x4, 9x3, 18x2, while all machines in 1xn, 2x3, 3x2, nx1 are decidable — Collatz-like behavior appears strictly between the decidable and universal frontiers.
key_facts.source: P. Michel, 'Simulation of the Collatz 3x+1 function by Turing machines', arXiv:1409.7322 (2014), downloaded PDF, Theorems 4.1, 5.1-5.4 and Section 1

key_facts.fact: BB(6) is now blocked by a concrete Collatz-like problem: 'Antihydra', a 6-state 2-symbol machine found 28 June 2024 (reported by mxdys on the bbchallenge Discord), iterates h <- h + floor(h/2) starting from h = 8, maintaining a counter c that gains +2 on even h and loses 1 on odd h; the machine halts iff c ever reaches -1 (i.e., iff at some point strictly more than twice as many odd as even iterates have occurred). Under the random-walk model (+2 with prob 1/2, -1 with prob 1/2, positive drift) the halting probability is astronomically small, so Antihydra 'probviously' runs forever, but proving non-halting is an open problem tied to equidistribution questions of the type of Mahler's Z-number problem for (3/2)^n.

Michel LMCS 2015, Section 1.3, on why busy beaver research must hit Collatz-like walls: 'the halting problem for Turing machines launched on a blank tape is m-complete, and this implies that this problem is as hard as the problem of the provability of a mathematical statement in a logical theory such as ZFC... When more and more non-halting Turing machines are studied to be proved non-halting, one has to expect to face hard open problems in mathematics, that is problems that current mathematical knowledge can't settle.' Related quantitative anchors: Yedidia-Aaronson 2016 constructed an explicit TM (originally 7910 states, improved by O'Rear to 748) whose non-halting is unprovable in ZFC (assuming a consistency-strength hypothesis); Aaronson conjectures ZF cannot prove the value of BB(20) (Conjecture 11) and PA cannot prove BB(10) (Conjecture 12), citing that 5- and 6-state machines 'can already engage number theory problems closely related to the Collatz Conjecture.'
key_facts.source: Michel LMCS 2015 Section 1.3 (downloaded); A. Yedidia and S. Aaronson, 'A relatively small Turing machine whose behavior is independent of set theory', Complex Systems 25(4), 2016, arXiv:1605.04343 (PDF in papers/); Aaronson, Busy Beaver Frontier, Section 5.2

key_facts.fact: Yolcu-Aaronson-Heule (the only systematic automated-deduction attack on Collatz) frame the logic results' practical import: 'Conway proved that a generalization of the problem is undecidable, which Kurtz and Simon later extended to a variant even closer to the Collatz conjecture. While the prevailing opinion is that the Collatz conjecture holds... the undecidability results help explain why this seems to be so hard to show.' Their method: encode Collatz as termination of string rewriting systems and search for matrix/arctic interpretations with SAT solvers; they can automatically prove termination for some Collatz variants (e.g., subsets of Farkas' variant) but not the full conjecture.
key_facts.source: E. Yolcu, S. Aaronson, M. J. X. Heule, 'An Automated Approach to the Collatz Conjecture', J. Automated Reasoning 67(15), 2023; arXiv:2105.14697 (downloaded)

key_facts.fact: Computational verification status (relevant to the Sigma^0_1 falsifiable components): the conjecture has been verified for all starting values up to 2^71 ≈ 2.36 x 10^21 (Barina, project ongoing as of 2025), and any nontrivial cycle must have length at least 217,976,794,617 (355,504,839,929 without the shortcut map). Conway 1972 cites verification to 10^9 (Lehmer/Selfridge); Conway 2013 cites Oliveira e Silva's 5 x 10^18 — the verified bound is itself a moving Pi^0_1 resource for cycle exclusion.
key_facts.source: Wikipedia, 'Collatz conjecture' (fetched 2026-07-20), citing D. Barina; Conway 1972; Conway 2013 citing T. Oliveira e Silva in the Lagarias volume

precise_statements: Conway 1972, Theorem: If f is any computable function, there is a function g such that (1) g(n)/n is periodic (with rational values), and (2) 2^f(n) = g^k(2^n), where k is minimal positive subject to g^k(2^n) being a power of 2. Corollary: There is no algorithm which, given a function g with g(n)/n periodic, and given a number n, determines whether or not there is k with g^k(n) = 1. ('Computable' = computable by a Minsky program, equivalent to (partial) recursive.)

Kurtz-Simon 2007, Definition 1.2: g is a Collatz function if there is an integer p >= 2 and rationals {a_i}, {b_i} (i < p) such that x ≡ i (mod p) implies g(x) = a_i x + b_i is integral. Theorem 1.4: Given a Collatz function g, it is undecidable whether or not for all integers x there exists an i such that g^(i)(x) = 1. Theorem 1.5: With range-g = {x : ∃i g^(i)(x) = 1}, the problem 'range-g = ω' is Π_2-complete (Π^0_2-complete).

Kurtz-Simon 2007, Theorem 2.3 (key lemma): A register machine M can be effectively converted into a register machine M' such that M is total if and only if M' reaches a halting configuration from every configuration. (Proved by a double simulation: an outer forward simulation plus an always-terminating inner simulation that checks the outer one for consistency before it may advance.)

Arithmetic-hierarchy decomposition (by quantifier inspection, anchored to Kurtz-Simon's formalization): Full Collatz conjecture ∀n>0 ∃k T^k(n)=1 is Π^0_2. 'There exists a nontrivial cycle' is Σ^0_1 (finite verifiable witness), hence 'no nontrivial cycles' is Π^0_1 — if this component is independent of a sound theory, it is TRUE. 'The orbit of n eventually enters a cycle' is Σ^0_1 in n (∃i<j T^i(n)=T^j(n)), hence 'no divergent orbits' is Π^0_2 — a false Π^0_2 sentence need not be refutable, so independence of the full conjecture from PA or ZFC would NOT imply its truth. Conway 2013: 'In general, if a Collatzian game does not stop, then there is no proof of this.'

De Mol 2008 (TCS 390:92-101): The Collatz problem reduces to the halting problem of the 2-tag system with 3 symbols and production rules a -> bc, b -> a, c -> aaa (deletion number v = 2), where n is encoded as a^n; the system computes the iterates of the shortcut Collatz map (n even -> n/2, n odd -> (3n+1)/2) and halts iff the Collatz orbit of n reaches 1. Any generalized Collatz function of modulus d is similarly simulated by a d-tag system.

Michel 2014, Theorem 5.3: The 3x+1 conjecture is true iff Turing machine M6 (5 states, 4 symbols) halts on all input x = x_k...x_0 representing a positive integer written in base 3. Theorem 4.1: the 3x+1 conjecture is true iff, for all positive integers x written in base 3, the never-halting 3-state 4-symbol machine M1 eventually reaches configuration ωb 0^n (A1) bω.

Michel 1993 / Aaronson 2020: The halting problem of the 5-state busy beaver champion (Marxen-Buntrock, runtime 47,176,870 = BB(5), proved by bbchallenge 2024-25) is governed by the Collatz-like map g(x) = (5x+18)/3 if x ≡ 0 (mod 3); (5x+22)/3 if x ≡ 1 (mod 3); halt if x ≡ 2 (mod 3), iterated from x = 0; the machine's halting-on-all-inputs problem is an open Collatz-like problem in number theory.

Conway 2013: There is an explicit FRACTRAN game with 24 fractions for which there are numbers n such that the game never stops, but this is not provable (in whatever consistent axiom system defines 'settleable'); improved to 7 fractions by John Rickard. Postscript (June 8, 2012): a positive-probability large-cycle argument (via mountains 2^k m - 1 -> 3^k m - 1 and CRT) makes it 'extremely unlikely that there can be a proof that the Collatz game has no cycles that contain only large numbers', and convinced Conway that the Collatz conjecture 'is itself very likely to be unsettleable'.
open_questions: Is the halting problem for the class TS(3,2) of tag systems (3 symbols, deletion number 2) — which contains De Mol's Collatz tag system — decidable? De Mol: proving TS(3,2) solvable 'might be very hard' since it would essentially settle Collatz. The decidability frontier value of the number of symbols mu for tag systems is still unknown.
Is Post's 1921 tag system (v=3: 0 -> 00, 1 -> 1101) decidable, and is its behavior formally reducible to/from a Collatz-like problem? (Suggested by Asveld, Hayes, De Mol; open for over a century.)

Can the Kurtz-Simon Pi^0_2-completeness be pushed down to structurally tiny classes of generalized Collatz functions (small fixed modulus, all a_i with the same denominator, or the multiplicative b_i = 0 case with fixed modulus), and is there any natural decidable subclass containing the actual 3x+1 function?

Is the Collatz conjecture provably equivalent (over PA) to some Pi^0_1 sentence — e.g., via a computable a priori bound on orbit excursions (an 'effective no-divergence' principle)? A positive answer would make independence-implies-truth apply to the whole conjecture; nothing of this kind is known.

Is there any non-heuristic evidence for or against independence of Collatz from PA or ZFC? Conway's 2013 unsettleability argument is explicitly probabilistic, not a theorem; no serious published proof program for independence exists. Related quantitative anchors: Aaronson's Conjectures 11 and 12 (ZF cannot prove BB(20); PA cannot prove BB(10)) remain open, as does his question whether the halting of all busy beaver champions can be reduced to Collatz-like statements.

Will the Antihydra problem (h <- h + floor(h/2) from h = 8; halt iff odd steps ever exceed twice the even steps plus one) — and hence BB(6) — be settled? This is the current concrete frontier where busy beaver research meets Collatz-like number theory, possibly connected to Mahler's Z-number/(3/2)^n equidistribution problem.

Michel's exponential Collatz-like functions f(dn+i) = (a_i p^n + b_i)/c_i (arising from the Kropitz 6-state champion): 'currently, no study of this type of function is known' — is there any theory of their termination?

Can automated termination proving (rewriting + matrix/arctic interpretations + SAT, per Yolcu-Aaronson-Heule) settle any Collatz variant of independent interest (e.g., Farkas' variant in full), and what is the weakest proof system that proves the partial results already known?
strategy_relevance: The computability results fence off the entire space of "generic" arguments: any technique strong enough to decide termination for all piecewise-linear residue-class maps is impossible (Conway 1972; Kurtz-Simon Pi^0_2-completeness), so a proof or disproof must exploit structure specific to (3x+1)/2 — Conway himself noted the theorem "says nothing about the Collatz game," and his Appendix 1 singles out circle-method-style arguments that convert the probabilistic drift heuristic into a theorem as the one non-inconceivable route. The hierarchy decomposition dictates asymmetric strategy: "no nontrivial cycles" is Pi^0_1 (true if independent, and attackable by the ever-growing 2^71 verification plus cycle-length bounds), whereas "no divergent orbits" is genuinely Pi^0_2, so independence results can never settle the full conjecture and a disproof-by-divergence could itself be unprovable — Conway's mountain/CRT postscript further warns that "no large cycles" may be true for reasons with no proof. For a disproof search or for calibrating difficulty, the busy-beaver corpus is the right laboratory: specific Collatz-like halting problems of nontrivial size do get settled (BB(5) and all Skelet machines, Coq-verified in 2024-25), while Antihydra marks exactly where current techniques fail, and De Mol's 3-rule tag system {a->bc, b->a, c->aaa} gives the minimal combinatorial presentation of Collatz on which to test rewriting/termination methods (Yolcu-Aaronson-Heule's SAT-based interpretation search being the state of the art).
